1: ʾāmar nābāl bĕlibbô ʾên ʾĕlōhîm — the fool has said in his heart, 'There is no God.' Nābāl (fool — not intellectual atheism but moral and practical rejection of God's involvement in human affairs; the nābāl is the person who acts as if God does not observe, evaluate, or respond to human behavior; cf. Abigail's description of Nabal, 1 Samuel 25:25). The denial is in the heart (bĕlibbô: in his heart) — an interior decision about divine governance that then shapes behavior. Psalm 53 is a variant of Psalm 14; the main difference is the use of ʾĕlōhîm (53) vs. YHWH (14) — the Elohistic variant. hisḥîtû wĕhiṭṭîbû ʿāwel — they are corrupt and have done vile deeds. Sāḥat (hiphil: to corrupt, act corruptly — from the Flood's vocabulary, Genesis 6:12). ʿĀwel (injustice, wrong — the practical social consequence of the interior denial of God).
2: ʾĕlōhîm mishshāmayim hishqîph — God looked down from heaven. Shāqaph (to look down from above — to peer over an edge from a height). The divine counter-survey: the fool claims there is no God; God looks down and surveys the fool's claim and the human reality beneath it. ʿal-bĕnê ʾādām lirʾôt hay-yēsh maskîl — on all humanity to see if anyone understands. Maskîl (one who understands, who acts wisely — from śākal: to have insight). The divine survey looking for a single maskîl — the most ironic title in the context: the psalm-title (maskîl) identifies the psalm itself as a wisdom-teaching, while the content reports the divine failure to find anyone with wisdom. dōrēsh ʾet-hāʾĕlōhîm — if anyone seeks after God. The seeking of God as the measure of understanding.
3: kullô sāg yāḥdāw neʾĕlāḥû — every single one has turned aside; together they have become rotten. Sûg (to turn back, withdraw — the apostasy of turning away from God). ʾĀlaḥ (niphal: to become rotten/putrid — of milk that has curdled, of something that has gone bad; the corruption reaching the point of decomposition). ʾên ʿōśēh-ṭôb ʾên gam-ʾeḥād — there is no one who does good — not a single person. The emphatic universal negation (gam-ʾeḥād: even one). Paul cites this in Romans 3:11-12 as scriptural proof of universal human sinfulness.
4: hălōʾ yādĕʿû pōʿălê ʾāwen — don't those who work evil know anything? Yādaʿ (to know — the rhetorical question: do they truly have no knowledge?). ʾōkĕlê ʿammî ʾākhĕlû leḥem — those who devour my people the way they eat bread. The image of the oppressors consuming (ʾākal: to eat) the people of God as if eating bread — casual, routine consumption of the vulnerable. ʾĕlōhîm lōʾ qārāʾû — and never call on God. The failure to call on God (qārāʾ) is both symptom and diagnosis: the evildoers do not pray because they do not believe God will or should respond.
5: shām pāḥădû-phāḥad lōʾ-hāyāh phāḥad — but there they are, overwhelmed with terror where there was no terror. The divine irony: the people who denied God's existence and operation experience overwhelming terror (pāḥad: fear, dread) where there was nothing to fear — the panic comes from nowhere visible because its source is the God who was denied. kî-ʾĕlōhîm pizzar ʿaṣmôt ḥōnākekhā — for God scattered the bones of those who attacked you. The specific historical reference (different from Ps 14) — a deliverance in which YHWH scattered the bones of the attackers. hĕbōshotāh kî-ʾĕlōhîm mĕʾāsām — you put them to shame, for God has rejected them. Mĕʾāsām (he has rejected them — from māʾas: to despise, reject; cf. Psalms 78:67 where Ephraim is rejected). The divine rejection as the theological explanation for the historical defeat.
6: mî yittēn miṣṣiyyôn yĕshûʿōt yisrāʾēl — oh, that Israel's rescue would come out of Zion! The closing eschatological prayer — mî yittēn (who will give? = O that! — the wish-formula expressing longing). Yĕshûʿōt (salvations, plural — the fullness of rescue). From Zion as the source (cf. Psalms 14:7 which is nearly identical). bĕshûb ʾĕlōhîm shĕbût ʿammô — when God restores the fortunes of his people. Shûb shĕbût/shĕbît (to restore the restoration — a figure of speech expressing complete reversal of captivity and distress; cf. Job 42:10; Jeremiah 29:14). yāgēl yaʿăqōb yiśmaḥ yisrāʾēl — Jacob will rejoice and Israel will be glad. The conclusion connects the wisdom-psalm's grim survey of universal foolishness and corruption with the hope of divine salvation: only YHWH's initiative from Zion can break the cycle.
